sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa dell'aumento delle tensioni geopolitiche tra USA e Iran, con il VIX che sale a 30.61 e il DXY che guadagna lo 0.05%. Il prezzo del petrolio è sceso del 4.78% a $107.39, ma rimane elevato. Le borse asiatiche sono state negative, con il Nikkei che ha perso lo 1.58%.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to rising geopolitical tensions between the US and Iran, with VIX increasing to 30.61 and DXY gaining 0.05%. Oil prices dropped 4.78% to $107.39 but remain elevated. Asian markets were negative, with Nikkei losing 1.58%.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato azionario è stato negativo, con l'S&P 500 che ha perso lo 0.39% e il TLT che è salito dell'1.33%. Il prezzo dell'oro è aumentato dell'1.44% a $4591.10. Il mercato delle criptovalute è stato misto, con Bitcoin che ha guadagnato l'1.07% e Ethereum che è salito dello 0.70%.
market_impact_summary.en: The stock market was negative, with S&P 500 losing 0.39% and TLT rising 1.33%. Gold prices increased 1.44% to $4591.10. The cryptocurrency market was mixed, with Bitcoin gaining 1.07% and Ethereum rising 0.70%.

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno a tutti, benvenuti nel nostro briefing finanziario di oggi, 31 marzo 2026. Iniziamo con un'analisi della situazione globale.

I mercati asiatici hanno chiuso la giornata in territorio negativo. Il Nikkei ha perso l'1.58%, chiudendo a 51,064 punti, mentre lo Shanghai ha ceduto lo 0.64%, terminando a 3898.28 punti. Il sentiment globale è di risk-off, con gli investitori prudenti in attesa di sviluppi macroeconomici cruciali.

In Europa, lo STOXX 600 ha guadagnato l'1.03%, raggiungendo 57.64 punti. Negli Usa, l'S&P 500 ha chiuso in calo del 0.39% a 6343.72 punti. Il Dollar Index (DXY) ha perso lo 0.05%, attestandosi a 100.46 punti. Il VIX, indice della volatilità, è sceso dell'1.42% a 30.61 punti. L'oro ha guadagnato l'1.44%, arrivando a 4591.10 dollari l'oncia. Il petrolio Brent è sceso del 4.78% a 107.39 dollari al barile. I tassi d'interesse sono in movimento, con il TLT Bond USA 20Y che guadagna l'1.33% a 86.78 punti e il US 10Y Yield che scende del 2.21% al 4.34%.

Sul fronte geopolitico, le tensioni tra Usa e Iran continuano a influenzare i mercati, con dichiarazioni del presidente Trump su possibili azioni contro l'Iran. In questo contesto, la Nato ha espresso preoccupazione per la stabilità nella regione. Macroeconomicamente, gli investitori attendono con ansia i prossimi dati sul PIL degli Usa, in programma per il 29 aprile. La BCE ha recentemente mantenuto i tassi d'interesse al 2.00%, segnale di attenzione verso l'inflazione nell'Eurozona.

Concludiamo per ora la nostra analisi dei mercati tradizionali e della macroeconomia. Tra poco, passeremo a esaminare il comparto digitale, con un'analisi approfondita delle criptovalute e degli ultimi sviluppi nel settore.

Passiamo ora al comparto degli asset digitali. Iniziamo con un deep dive su Bitcoin. La criptovaluta ha registrato un leggero aumento dello 0.14%, raggiungendo il prezzo di $67,396. Gli ETF su Bitcoin hanno registrato un afflusso giornaliero di $69.4M. 

  Spostando l'attenzione sulle altcoin, Ethereum è salita dello 0.70% a $2,058, mentre Solana è scesa dello 0.34% a $83.16. Binance Coin ha registrato una diminuzione dello 0.69% a $611.24.

  Per quanto riguarda il sentiment, l'indice Fear & Greed è a 11, in territorio di Estrema Paura. Questo livello di paura estrema potrebbe essere un segnale di acquisto per gli investitori più esperti. La correlazione con i dati macroeconomici statunitensi, come il recente tasso di inflazione e il mercato del lavoro, potrebbe influenzare ulteriormente il mercato delle criptovalute.

Grazie per l'attenzione e a domani
audio_script_en:
Good morning and welcome to today's financial update.

Global sentiment is cautious, with investors monitoring geopolitical tensions. Key data points include a decline in US Non-Farm Payrolls to -92,000 and a slight decrease in the US Core CPI Year over Year to 2.7%.

In traditional markets, equities experienced a downturn. The S&P 500 closed at 6,343.72, down 0.39%. The STOXX 600 saw a gain of 1.03%, closing at 57.64. The Nikkei ended at 51,064, down 1.58%, while the Shanghai index closed at 3,898.28, down 0.64%. The Hang Seng index in Hong Kong finished at 24,651, down 0.40%. In the bond market, the US 10-year yield decreased to 4.34%, down 2.21%. The TLT Bond USA 20Y increased to 86.78, up 1.33%. In currencies, the Euro to US Dollar exchange rate was 1.14, down 0.20%. The Dollar Index stood at 100.46, down 0.05%. Commodities saw gold rising to $4,591.10 per ounce, up 1.44%, while Brent crude fell to $107.39, down 4.78%.

Geopolitical tensions are on the rise, with potential implications for inflation and interest rates. The recent developments between the US and Iran have sparked concerns about oil supplies and nuclear proliferation. In the US, the PCE Core Year over Year stands at 3.1%. The Federal Reserve's current funds rate is 3.64%. The Eurozone's GDP Quarter over Quarter growth was 0.2%. The European Central Bank recently adjusted its interest rate to 2.00%. Upcoming data releases include the US GDP Quarter over Quarter and Eurozone CPI, which will be crucial for understanding the economic trajectory and potential central bank responses.

Moving on to the digital assets space...

Let's dive deeper into Bitcoin. The recent BTC ETF daily net inflow of $69.4M suggests continued institutional interest. From a technical standpoint, Bitcoin's price is holding steady at $67,396, up 0.14% today. The hashrate has seen a decline following the Iran conflict, which could have implications for mining operations.

Let's pivot to the cryptocurrency markets and take a look at some altcoins. Ethereum is trading at $2,058, up 0.70%, while Solana is at $83.16, down 0.34%. Binance Coin is currently at $611.24, down 0.69%. These movements reflect the ongoing volatility in the altcoin market.

In terms of market sentiment, the Fear & Greed Index is at 11, indicating extreme fear. This is often a sign that investors are overly cautious, which can lead to buying opportunities. Historically, such periods of extreme fear have been followed by market rebounds. The correlation with traditional markets remains a factor, especially given the recent macroeconomic data releases. 

Overall, the crypto market continues to navigate through a complex landscape of inflows, technical trends, and regulatory discussions.

Thanks for tuning in. We'll be back tomorrow with another market update. Until then, stay informed and stay ahead. A quick look at what's on the radar for tomorrow and we'll wrap up here. Have a great day and we'll catch you soon. Goodbye.

articles.title: The Iran war risks triggering a new wave of nuclear proliferation
articles.snippet: The Iran war risks triggering a new wave of nuclear proliferation Expert comment thilton.drupal 30 March 2026 States may be tempted to pursue their own nuclear weapons as they seek deterrence against attacks amid uncertainty over US guarantees. The US-Israeli war with Iran is taking place as the global non-proliferation regime is already under significant strain. New START, the last bilateral nuclear arms control treaty between the US and Russia, expired in February with nothing to replace it. China is currently expanding and modernizing its nuclear arsenal. France has announced an expansion of its nuclear programme and closer cooperation with European partners. Public opinion in several non-nuclear states such as Turkey, Poland and South Korea seems to be shifting towards support for deve

articles.title_en: The Iran war risks triggering a new wave of nuclear proliferation
articles.summary_it: L'articolo discute come la guerra in Iran potrebbe scatenare una nuova ondata di proliferazione nucleare, poiché gli stati potrebbero essere tentati di perseguire le proprie armi nucleari.
articles.summary_en: The article discusses how the Iran war could trigger a new wave of nuclear proliferation, as states may be tempted to pursue their own nuclear weapons.

articles.title: The realities of economic security
articles.snippet: The Peterson Institute for International Economics presents a high-level analytical discussion of the realities of economic security, examining real-world trade-offs and vulnerabilities in today's geopolitically fractured world. Anjali Bhatt, host of PIIE Insider Live, moderates a candid discussion with PIIE experts: Warwick McKibbin, who presents global macroeconomic scenarios of the impact of the Middle East conflict; Cullen Hendrix, who discusses vulnerabilities of the global economy to the disrupted supply of commodities including food, fuel, and industrial inputs; and Jacob Funk…

articles.title: Successes and Setbacks
articles.snippet: Welcome to The Ukraine Compass, a weekly digest of Ukrainian commentary and analysis from across the political spectrum only for War on the Rocks members. Each Monday, we bring you a curated selection of articles from Ukrainian media offering insight into how Ukrainians themselves debate the issues shaping their country.American coverage often narrows the view to the battlefield — these pieces widen it, revealing the texture of daily life, politics, and public argument in a nation at war. The perspectives gathered here are varied, candid, and often surprising, together forming a more complete picture of Ukraine as it really is.Frontline and StrategyМежа — The post Successes and Setbacks appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Putin Is Not Trapped: Why Regime Survival Does Not Depend on Victory
articles.snippet: For four years, analysts and policymakers have warned that Russian President Vladimir Putin “cannot afford to lose” in Ukraine. Increasingly, some argue he cannot afford peace either. In this view, the war is existential for his regime. Defeat would shatter Putin’s legacy and potentially end his rule. Trapped between humiliation and collapse, inviting comparisons to the fate of the last Russian tsar, Nicholas II, Putin is portrayed as having no viable off-ramp from an unwinnable war.These assumptions were central to Biden-era debates over escalation management. The Biden administration had concerns that excessive pressure might destabilize the regime, thus triggering escalation, The post Putin Is Not Trapped: Why Regime Survival Does Not Depend on Victory appeared first on War on the Rocks

articles.title: Coal Is Back — and Japan Is Driving the Rally
articles.snippet: Japan’s heavy dependence on imported energy is being put through a real-time stress test. The crisis triggered by the war in Iran and the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has exposed the structural vulnerabilities in one of the world’s largest energy importers. With roughly 90% of its crude oil sourced from the Middle East, Tokyo has already moved to release around 80 million barrels from its strategic petroleum reserves – equivalent to roughly 26 days of domestic oil demand. This should be sufficient to stabilize the…

articles.title: Oil Spike Collides With Fragile Global Growth
articles.snippet: The worst energy crisis in history is sending ripples across global markets, and optimism is in short supply. IEA’s chief said the current crisis is worse than all previous ones put together. JP Morgan analysts warned Asia is going to be hit the hardest, although other analysts see Europe as suffering the most from the fallout. And yet it seems the seriousness of the situation has yet to sink in. The European Central Bank’s president, Christine Lagarde, said in a recent interview with The Economist that the risks stemming from the U.S.…

articles.title: Three Crises, Four Years: Europe Keeps Failing the Same Energy Test
articles.snippet: For the third time in four years, Europe is waking up to discover that it has sleepwalked into yet another energy crisis." When Russia illegally invaded Ukraine in February of 2022, Europe was caught in an extremely compromised position, as it was dependent on Russian producers for 40 percent of its natural gas. When energy sanctions were slapped on the Kremlin, the impact on European energy markets was dire, with many families plunging into energy poverty. In the years since that crisis, European leaders have made efforts to diversify their energy…

articles.title: Bangladesh Seeks U.S. Waiver for Russian Diesel as IMF Warns of Energy Shock
articles.snippet: Bangladesh has formally requested a temporary sanctions waiver from the United States to import roughly 600,000 metric tonnes of Russian diesel to cover two months of demand as its energy crisis intensifies, Reuters reported on Monday, with officials pointing to Washington’s recent 30-day waiver granted to India as precedent. The move comes as the International Monetary Fund (IMF) warns the Iran war is driving a global energy shock, with disrupted oil flows pushing up fuel costs, tightening financial conditions, and placing acute pressure…

articles.title: Powell: Fed Will 'Wait and See' Before Pulling Interest Rate Trigger
articles.snippet: Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell on Monday said that U.S. monetary policy is in a good place, and the Fed will wait and see how the ongoing Middle East conflict will impact the economy and inflation before taking further action on interest rates. Powell reiterated that it is "too soon to know" the long-term scope and duration of the war's economic effects, noting that geopolitical risks make the outlook uncertain. According to the Fed chair, energy shocks tend to be temporary, and raising interest rates immediately to combat inflation caused…

articles.title: U.S. Senators Unveil ‘Mined in America Act’ to Reshore BTC Mining, Codify Bitcoin Strategic Reserve
articles.snippet: Bitcoin Magazine U.S. Senators Unveil ‘Mined in America Act’ to Reshore BTC Mining, Codify Bitcoin Strategic Reserve Senators Bill Cassidy and Cynthia Lummis introduced the “Mined in America Act” to reshore U.S. bitcoin mining, reduce reliance on Chinese hardware, and codify a Strategic Bitcoin Reserve into law. This post U.S. Senators Unveil ‘Mined in America Act’ to Reshore BTC Mining, Codify Bitcoin Strategic Reserve first appeared on Bitcoin Magazine and is written by Micah Zimmerman .
